title: US-Iran Naval Standoff Rattles Oil Markets as Tensions Escalate

blocks.content: Escalating military tensions between the United States and Iran have gripped markets, sending crude prices swinging as investors weigh the risk of a prolonged blockade against ceasefire talks.

The White House is defending a naval blockade of Iranian ports as crude futures fell sharply following a surprise peace proposal from Tehran. President Trump rejected the overture, warning that military operations will continue until Iranian threats are eliminated. The standoff has injected fresh volatility into energy markets already navigating supply uncertainty.

For investors, the immediate focus is on whether higher oil prices will stick. Initial declines suggest markets view the standoff as manageable for now, but a miscalculation could send crude spiking and complicate the Federal Reserve's already uncertain rate path. Energy stocks, defense contractors, and airline shares remain particularly exposed to sustained escalation.
• S&P 500: Little changed as gains in defense and energy offset declines in rate-sensitive sectors.
• FTSE 100: Flat as commodity-linked stocks steadied after oil's initial drop.
• Nasdaq: Slight gain as tech held support despite geopolitical headwinds.
• US-IRAN CONFLICT: Crude futures fell on news of Iran's peace proposal, though oil markets remain on edge with the naval blockade intact.
• SPIRIT AIRLINES: The budget carrier is shutting down immediately after a last-minute rescue deal collapsed, leaving passengers stranded and consolidating the low-cost airline market.
• BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY: Warren Buffett revealed a record $397 billion cash pile as operating earnings doubled, signaling patience ahead.
• FEDERAL RESERVE: Chairman Powell faced unprecedented dissent at the FOMC meeting, with updated rate projections adding to uncertainty over the policy path.
Any escalation or de-escalation in the US-Iran standoff will dominate early trading, with crude and defense stocks leading sentiment shifts.
